steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The text argues that doctors' credentials were outweighed by participants' sense of the experiential relevance of the messengers. Former trial volunteers had lived through what participants were considering; doctors had not. The answer must explain why shared experience mattered more than credentials.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Participants did not have enough experience to evaluate the credibility of the doctors’ messages but did have enough experience to evaluate the credibility of former trial volunteers’ messages.: shifts focus to participants' own prior experience — not what the passage is arguing. ✗
  • Choice B — The fact that former trial volunteers went through the same experience that participants were contemplating while doctors did not was more important to participants than the doctors’ status as credentialed experts was.: directly captures the relevance argument — former volunteers' shared experience resonated more with participants than doctors' professional credentials. ✓
  • Choice C — Participants regarded the experiences of both the doctors and former trial volunteers as relevant to the subject of clinical trials but were skeptical of the doctors’ status as credentialed experts.: claims doctors' experience was relevant — contradicts the text, which says credentials were outweighed. ✗
  • Choice D — Messages from former trial volunteers depicted clinical trials as being more positive experiences than did messages from doctors.: introduces the tone of messages — the text does not discuss tone. ✗
